text: The surgeon demonstrates a robotic transhiatal esophagectomy, retracting the hiatus anteriorly and on the right side. Station 111 lymph node is excised with the specimen, revealing a clear dissection plane between the specimen and the pericardium. Fibro-fatty tissue is removed with the specimen, creating a distinct plane between the specimen and the mediastinal pleura. The surgical field is cleared by dissecting the mesoesophagus from the left and right sides of the mediastinal pleura. The mesoesophagus is lifted anteriorly, establishing a plane between it and the descending aorta.

The surgeon performs robotic trans-hiatal esophagectomy, focusing on the right anterolateral surgical field. The right side periosteovisal tissue is dissected, and lymph node basins are removed with the specimen. The procedure involves creating three distinct surgical fields: right anterolateral, left anterolateral, and posterior, each handled delicately.

The surgeon separates the mesoesophagus and perisophageal tissue from the left side of the mediastinal pleura, revealing the descending aorta below. The dissection progresses towards the arch of the aorta, with the pericardium visible anteriorly and the descending aorta posteriorly. Mesoesophageal tissue and lymph nodes are included with the esophagus specimen. As the dissection approaches the aortic arch, branches of the left vagus nerve are encountered and removed. The autopulmonary window, containing soft tissue and lymph nodes, is identified as part of the specimen.

The surgeon uses monopolar scissors for sharp dissection to separate the esophageal tissue from the left mediastinal pleura, the downward arch of the aorta, and anteriorly from the main pulmonary trunk and pericardium. The main trunk of the left vagus nerve is cut, releasing the specimen. Similar steps are repeated on the right side, where the right vagus nerve is identified and cut, allowing access to the right station 109 lymph node basin, which is taken with the specimen.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the station 107 subcarinal lymph node, ensuring minimal bleeding to maintain a clear surgical field. The right bronchial artery is visible anteriorly, serving as a landmark for defining the right bronchus. The dissection proceeds flush to the esophagus, avoiding supra azygous lymph node clearance. The station 109 lymph node is identified and removed, revealing the inferior border of the left bronchus beneath it. Mesoesophageal tissue is taken with the specimen.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection during the robotic transhiatal esophagectomy, revealing the azygos vein and thoracic duct running parallel on the right side of the specimen. The descending aorta is visible on the left side, with an avascular plane between the prevascular fascia covering major vessels and the visceral fascia covering the specimen. The thoracic duct is identified as an important landmark embedded within the posterior fat, leading up to the thoracic inlet.
